## Per-question answers

### `-7013890438520559398`
> **Pelé has the highest goals in world football with 1281 goals recognized by FIFA**
> Pelé's 1281 goals are recognized by FIFA as the highest total achieved by a professional footballer. [alce::-7013890438520559398::p0]
> Although the Soccer Statistic Foundation (rssf) recognizes only 767 goals in official mode, occupying the third place after Josef Bican (805) and Romario (772), FIFA's recognition of Pelé's 1281 goals represents the highest acknowledged total. [alce::-7013890438520559398::p0]

Cited: alce::-7013890438520559398::p0

### `7089015503030534342`
> **Simon & Garfunkel**
> "The Sound of Silence", originally "The Sounds of Silence", is a song by the American music duo Simon & Garfunkel. [alce::7089015503030534342::p0]
> The song was written by Paul Simon over a period of several months in 1963 and 1964. [alce::7089015503030534342::p0]

Cited: alce::7089015503030534342::p0

### `8793099883447006698`
> **June 29, 2007**
> The first iPhone was released on June 29, 2007. [alce::8793099883447006698::p0, alce::8793099883447006698::p4]
> It was officially announced on January 9, 2007, before being released later that year. [alce::8793099883447006698::p0]

### `-881464876144297194`
> **James and Oliver Phelps played Fred and George Weasley; Chris Rankin played Percy Weasley**
> James and Oliver Phelps are identical twin British actors, best known for playing Fred and George Weasley in the 'Harry Potter' film series. [alce::-881464876144297194::p0]
> Chris Rankin won the role of Percy Weasley in September 2000 and appeared in the Harry Potter series as a brother of Harry Potter's best friend, Ron Weasley. [alce::-881464876144297194::p1]

Cited: alce::-881464876144297194::p0, alce::-881464876144297194::p1

### `2378678654868379935`
> **2Cellos, Dua Lipa, and Sean Paul performed at the 2018 Champions League Final**
> 2Cellos performed the UEFA Champions League Anthem ahead of the 2018 UEFA Champions League Final between Real Madrid C.F. and Liverpool F.C. [alce::2378678654868379935::p37]
> Dua Lipa performed at the opening ceremony preceding the final, with Jamaican rapper Sean Paul joining her as a special guest to perform their collaborative song, 'No Lie'. [alce::2378678654868379935::p1]
> Ukrainian singer Tayanna also performed at the opening ceremony preceding the final. [alce::2378678654868379935::p9]

Cited: alce::2378678654868379935::p1, alce::2378678654868379935::p37, alce::2378678654868379935::p9

### `-3322598412088356524`
> **Louise shot and killed the man who attempted to rape Thelma**
> A man attempts to rape Thelma, but as the women walk away, he yells that he should have continued the rape and continually insults them. [alce::-3322598412088356524::p79]
> Enraged, Louise shoots him dead, and the pair flee the scene. [alce::-3322598412088356524::p79]

### `6962706727076805207`
> **A bait car (also called a decoy car, hot car, or trap car)**
> A bait car, also called a decoy car, hot car or trap car, is a vehicle used by law enforcement agencies to capture car thieves or thieves who steal items from cars. [alce::6962706727076805207::p0]
> The vehicles are modified with audio/video surveillance technology, and can be remotely monitored and controlled. [alce::6962706727076805207::p0]
> Those set up to catch car thieves may include GPS tracking and a 'kill switch' may be installed in the vehicle allowing police to remotely disable the engine and lock all doors remotely, preventing escape. [alce::6962706727076805207::p0]

Cited: alce::6962706727076805207::p0

### `-8113348361974619629`
> **The 12th day of Christmas (Twelfth Night) is January 5**
> The Twelve Days of Christmas begin on Christmas Day (December 25) and end on January 5, eve of the traditional date of the Epiphany. [alce::-8113348361974619629::p12]
> In most Western ecclesiastical traditions, 'Christmas Day' is considered the 'First Day of Christmas' and the Twelve Days are 25 December – 5 January, inclusive. [alce::-8113348361974619629::p2]
> The 12th day is named as Twelfth Night, which falls on January 5. [alce::-8113348361974619629::p13]

### `1029755223764707835`
> **Multiple authors including Jewish Christians, Luke, John the Apostle, and others**
> The books of the New Testament were all or nearly all written by Jewish Christians—that is, Jewish disciples of Christ, who lived in the Roman Empire, and under Roman occupation. [alce::1029755223764707835::p1]
> Luke wrote the Gospel of Luke and the Book of Acts, though scholars are divided as to whether Luke was a Gentile or a Hellenistic Jew. [alce::1029755223764707835::p1]
> Church tradition has held that John the Apostle is the author of the Gospel of John and four other books of the New Testament — the three Epistles of John and the Book of Revelation. [alce::1029755223764707835::p55]
> The Gospels were written between 68-110 CE, Acts between 95-110, Epistles between 51-110 CE and Revelation in c. 95 CE. [alce::1029755223764707835::p10]

Cited: alce::1029755223764707835::p1, alce::1029755223764707835::p10, alce::1029755223764707835::p55

> The repeated sentences or clauses provides emphasis to a center theme or idea the author is trying to convey. [alce::-4891954000928831702::p8]

> In Mexico it is used in the festivities of the Day of the Dead, to decorate altars and tombs; hence the name 'flower of the dead'. [alce::-2046820450216612197::p14]
> In modern Mexico the marigold is sometimes called 'Flor de Muerto' (Flower of Dead). [alce::-2046820450216612197::p0]
> The marigold was regarded as the flower of the dead in pre-Hispanic Mexico, parallel to the lily in Europe, and is still widely used in the Day of the Dead celebrations. [alce::-2046820450216612197::p67]

### `4965896127144803833`
> **Multiple factors led to the downfall of the Inca Empire: Spanish conquest, internal civil war, epidemic disease, and the empire's prior decline**
> While the Spanish conquest was undoubtedly the proximate cause of the collapse of the Inca Empire, it may very well have been past its peak and already in the process of decline. [alce::4965896127144803833::p4]
> Smallpox, introduced from Panama and preceding the Spanish conquerors, swept through the Inca Empire in the years between 1524 and 1526, and the death of the Incan ruler Huayna Capac and most of his family caused the fall of the Incan political structure and contributed to the civil war between the brothers Atahualpa and Huáscar. [alce::4965896127144803833::p52]
> During the Spanish conquest, the Spaniard Francisco Pizarro captured Atahualpa and used him to control the Inca Empire, and eventually the Spanish executed Atahualpa, effectively ending the empire. [alce::4965896127144803833::p85]
> The indigenous population dramatically collapsed due to exploitation, socioeconomic change and epidemic diseases introduced by the Spanish. [alce::4965896127144803833::p2]

Cited: alce::4965896127144803833::p2, alce::4965896127144803833::p4, alce::4965896127144803833::p52, alce::4965896127144803833::p85

### `-5001543574321430501`
> **Chicago blues - an electric blues style of urban blues**
> Chicago blues is a form of blues music indigenous to Chicago, Illinois, and is an electric blues style of urban blues. [alce::-5001543574321430501::p1]
> As blues moved north to Chicago during the Great Migration, the electric style developed earlier in Electric Memphis and Texas Blues remained, but became harder and edgier, with instruments like the saxophone incorporated into the music. [alce::-5001543574321430501::p35]
> In the late 1950s, the West Side style blues emerged in Chicago, incorporating elements of blues rock with a greater emphasis on standards and traditional blues song forms, dominated by amplified electric lead guitar. [alce::-5001543574321430501::p14]

### `4421895045446492516`
> **Peristalsis**
> The movement of food in the food pipe (esophagus) is aided by peristaltic contractions, which are radially symmetrical contractions and relaxations of muscles that propagate in a wave down the tube. [alce::4421895045446492516::p3, alce::4421895045446492516::p5]
> Peristaltic movement comprises relaxation of circular smooth muscles, then their contraction behind the chewed material to keep it from moving backward, then longitudinal contraction to push it forward. [alce::4421895045446492516::p3]

Cited: alce::4421895045446492516::p3, alce::4421895045446492516::p5

> **King John significantly rebuilt and enhanced Kenilworth Castle, spending £1,115 between 1210 and 1216 on construction and improvements**

> He also significantly improved the castle's water defences by damming the Finham and Inchford Brooks, creating the Great Mere, which turned Kenilworth into one of the largest English castles of the time with one of the largest artificial lake defences in England. ### `-4488762816493062068`
> **Thomas Malory**

### `2215574890678862752`
> **Multiple scientists contributed to tuberculosis cures: Robert Koch introduced tuberculin in 1890, Weleminsky developed tuberculomucin in 1912, and Albert Schatz, Elizabeth Bugie, and Selman Waksman isolated streptomycin in 1944, which was the first effective antibiotic cure.**
> At the Tenth International Medical Congress held 1890 in Berlin, Koch unexpectedly introduced a cure for tuberculosis, which he called tuberculin. [alce::2215574890678862752::p1]
> In 1912 Weleminsky published his discovery of a new treatment for tuberculosis, which he named tuberculomucin (Tbm). [alce::2215574890678862752::p3]
> In 1944 Albert Schatz, Elizabeth Bugie, and Selman Waksman isolated streptomycin produced by a bacterial strain 'Streptomyces griseus.' Streptomycin was the first effective antibiotic against 'M. tuberculosis'. [alce::2215574890678862752::p16]

Cited: alce::2215574890678862752::p1, alce::2215574890678862752::p16, alce::2215574890678862752::p3

> **The skeletal muscle fibers innervated by that motor neuron's axonal terminals**
> A motor unit is made up of a motor neuron and the skeletal muscle fibers innervated by that motor neuron's axonal terminals. [alce::1719387094791600068::p6]
> Each individual muscle fiber is innervated by only one motor neuron, but one motor neuron may innervate several muscle fibers. [alce::1719387094791600068::p34]
> In vertebrates, one neuron controls a group of muscle fibers. [alce::1719387094791600068::p17]

Cited: alce::1719387094791600068::p17, alce::1719387094791600068::p34, alce::1719387094791600068::p6

### `8216960630679687356`
> **John L. O'Sullivan coined the phrase "Manifest Destiny" in 1845, which refers to the belief that the United States was destined to expand across the North American continent as part of Providence's design.**
> In July 1845, the New York newspaper editor John L. O'Sullivan coined the phrase, 'Manifest Destiny,' as the 'design of Providence' supporting the territorial expansion of the United States. [alce::8216960630679687356::p1]
> Manifest Destiny was a justification for expansion and westward movement, or, in some interpretations, an ideology or doctrine that helped to promote the progress of civilization. [alce::8216960630679687356::p1]
> 'Manifest Destiny', a term coined by journalist John O'Sullivan, captured the idea that the young American nation was destined to rule all of the North American continent. [alce::8216960630679687356::p77]

Cited: alce::8216960630679687356::p1, alce::8216960630679687356::p77

### `-7777910189889414373`
> **The Paschal candle is lit at Easter, during baptisms, and during funeral services**
> A new Paschal candle is blessed and lit every year at Easter, and is used throughout the Paschal season which is during Easter and then throughout the year on special occasions, such as baptisms and funerals. [alce::-7777910189889414373::p0]
> The Paschal candle is also lit during baptisms to signify the Holy Spirit and fire that John the Baptist promised to those who were baptised in Christ. [alce::-7777910189889414373::p5]
> The Paschal candle is also lit and placed near the casket or urn during funeral services such as the Mass of Repose. [alce::-7777910189889414373::p5]
> At the beginning of the Easter Vigil Mass, the Paschal Candle is lit directly from the Paschal fire. [alce::-7777910189889414373::p9]

Cited: alce::-7777910189889414373::p0, alce::-7777910189889414373::p5, alce::-7777910189889414373::p9

### `-9150237805953541495`
> **Emory S. Bogardus**
> The Bogardus social distance scale is a psychological testing scale created by Emory S. Bogardus to empirically measure people's willingness to participate in social contacts of varying degrees of closeness with members of diverse social groups, such as racial and ethnic groups. [alce::-9150237805953541495::p6]
> For Bogardus, social distance is a function of affective distance between the members of two groups, and for him, social distance is essentially a measure of how much or little sympathy the members of a group feel for another group. [alce::-9150237805953541495::p1]

Cited: alce::-9150237805953541495::p1, alce::-9150237805953541495::p6

### `-2777825999784077693`
> **Karl Landsteiner**
> The ABO blood types were discovered by Karl Landsteiner in 1901, for which he received the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 1930. [alce::-2777825999784077693::p1]
> In 1900, he found that blood sera from different persons would clump together (agglutinate) when mixed, and in 1901 he identified the three blood groups A, B and O. [alce::-2777825999784077693::p1, alce::-2777825999784077693::p11]
> Landsteiner's work made it possible to determine blood group and allowed a way for blood transfusions to be carried out much more safely. [alce::-2777825999784077693::p3]

Cited: alce::-2777825999784077693::p1, alce::-2777825999784077693::p11, alce::-2777825999784077693::p3

### `-673487982339290600`
> **1778**
> France signed an alliance with the Americans in 1778, and sending an army and navy that turned the American Revolution into a world war. [alce::-673487982339290600::p23]
> France offered support to rebel American colonists seeking independence from Britain during the American Revolutionary War and in 1778 entered the war on their side. [alce::-673487982339290600::p10]
> France supplied the colonists with covert aid, but remained officially neutral until 1778. The Treaty of Alliance (1778) established the Franco-American alliance, and France entered the war on behalf of the rebels and against Great Britain. [alce::-673487982339290600::p41]

### `4537952778249650608`
> **Polar latitudes (near the poles)**
> Near the poles, which coincide with the rotational axis of Earth as it passes through the surface, the seasonal variations in the length of daytime are extreme. [alce::4537952778249650608::p23]
> Within 23.5° latitude of the poles, there will be at least some days each year during which the sun never goes below the horizon, and there will also be days when the Sun never rises above the horizon. [alce::4537952778249650608::p23]
> At middle latitudes, far from both the Equator and the poles, variations in the length of daytime are moderate. [alce::4537952778249650608::p0]

Cited: alce::4537952778249650608::p0, alce::4537952778249650608::p23

### `6630776554188306922`
> **The smell of vanilla comes from vanillin and related compounds in vanilla orchid seed pods**
> Vanilla is a flavoring derived from orchids of the genus 'Vanilla', primarily from the Mexican species, flat-leaved vanilla (V. planifolia). [alce::6630776554188306922::p2]
> Vanillin (4-hydroxy-3-methoxybenzaldehyde) is a major contributor to the characteristic flavor and aroma of real vanilla and is the main flavor component of cured vanilla beans. [alce::6630776554188306922::p6]
> Residues of vanillin and 4-hydroxybenzaldehyde – the main aroma and flavor components of vanilla – were found by archeologists in burial jugs of a Bronze Age tomb in Megiddo, Israel. [alce::6630776554188306922::p1]

> **The Romano-Britons expelled the Romans from Britain**

[alce::-3499058328741896914::p1]

### `5568550634362402282`
> **At least 4 NBA players have scored 60 points in a game**
> James Harden became the first player in NBA history to score 60 points as part of a triple-double on January 30. [alce::5568550634362402282::p1]
> Klay Thompson scored 60 points on December 5, 2016, becoming the first player in NBA history to score 60 points in less than 30 minutes of action. [alce::5568550634362402282::p44]
> Kobe Bryant scored an NBA season-high 60 points against Utah in his last NBA game on April 13, becoming the oldest player to score 60 or more points in a game at 37 years old. [alce::5568550634362402282::p21]
> Kobe Bryant scored at least 60 points six times in his career. [alce::5568550634362402282::p96]
> Devin Booker, at age 20, became the youngest player to score 70 (or even 60) points in a game, with the Suns' previous scoring record of 60 points held by Tom Chambers on March 24, 1990. [alce::5568550634362402282::p29]

### `16881061444712536`
> **The Amazon River starts in the Peruvian Andes (at the Cordillera Rumi Cruz/Kiwicha snowfield at the headwaters of the Mantaro River) and finishes at the Atlantic Ocean**
> The headwaters of the Apurímac River on Nevado Mismi had been considered for nearly a century as the Amazon's most distant source, until a 2014 study found it to be the Cordillera Rumi Cruz at the headwaters of the Mantaro River in Peru. [alce::16881061444712536::p0]
> The most distant point in the Mantaro drainage is nearly 80 km farther upstream compared to Mt. Mismi in the Apurímac drainage, and thus the maximal length of the Amazon river is about 80 km longer than previously thought. [alce::16881061444712536::p14]
> The Marañon River flows to the Atlantic Ocean, and nowadays the Marañon River is generally thought to end at the confluence with the Ucayali River, after which most cartographers label the ensuing waterway the Amazon River. [alce::16881061444712536::p19]

### `-3553537514837674455`
> **Endothelial cells, astrocytes, and pericytes**
> The blood–brain barrier is formed by endothelial cells of the capillary wall, astrocyte end-feet ensheathing the capillary, and pericytes embedded in the capillary basement membrane. [alce::-3553537514837674455::p0, alce::-3553537514837674455::p1]
> Pericytes are responsible for tight junction formation and vesicle trafficking amongst endothelial cells and allow the formation of the blood–brain barrier by inhibiting the effects of various factors. [alce::-3553537514837674455::p12]
> Pericytes stabilize and monitor the maturation of endothelial cells by means of direct communication between the cell membrane as well as through paracrine signaling. [alce::-3553537514837674455::p13]

### `-5942352520455511076`
> **Adipose tissue is located beneath the skin (subcutaneous fat), around internal organs (visceral fat), in bone marrow (yellow bone marrow), intermuscular, and in breast tissue. In infants, brown adipose tissue is also found in interscapular, supraclavicular, suprarenal, pericardial, para-aortic regions and around the pancreas, kidney and trachea. In adults, brown adipose tissue depots are most often detected in supraclavicular, paravertebral, mediastinal, para-aortic and suprarenal regions.**
> In humans, adipose tissue is located: beneath the skin (subcutaneous fat), around internal organs (visceral fat), in bone marrow (yellow bone marrow), intermuscular and in the breast tissue. [alce::-5942352520455511076::p0]
> In infants, brown adipose tissue depots include, but are not limited to: interscapular, supraclavicular, suprarenal, pericardial, para-aortic and around the pancreas, kidney and trachea. [alce::-5942352520455511076::p5]
> In adults, the depots that are most often detected in FDG-PET scans are the supraclavicular, paravertebral, mediastinal, para-aortic and suprarenal ones. [alce::-5942352520455511076::p5]

Cited: alce::-5942352520455511076::p0, alce::-5942352520455511076::p5

### `7754436520813105788`
> **The sinoatrial node, located in the wall of the right atrium**

> In a healthy heart, the SA node continuously produces action potentials, setting the rhythm of the heart and is known as the heart's natural pacemaker. [alce::7754436520813105788::p3]

> **France was occupied by Germany starting in June 1940, with the German invasion beginning on May 10, 1940, Paris entered on June 14, 1940, and an armistice signed on June 22, 1940**

> The German invasion army entered Paris on 14 June 1940, following the battle of France. [alce::-596092551637359468::p0]
> Under the terms of the agreement, Germany occupied two-thirds of France, including Paris. [alce::-596092551637359468::p0]

### `6757588787784107632`
> **Plywood came into use in furniture in the 1940s, with the Eames plywood furniture being produced from 1946 onwards**
> The modern version of plywood was invented in the US in 1905 in Portland, Oregon. [alce::6757588787784107632::p0]
> In 1915, Henry L. Haskell innovated a method to mold plywood into three dimensional shapes using heat, hydraulic pressure and his patented waterproof glue, which enabled its use in furniture applications. [alce::6757588787784107632::p14]
> The Eames plywood furniture chair was produced from 1946 until 1947 by Evans Molded Plywood of Venice Beach, California for the Herman Miller furniture company, representing a striking new way of looking at furniture design. [alce::6757588787784107632::p11]
> Designers Charles and Ray Eames are known for their plywood-based furniture, as is Finnish Architect Alvar Aalto and his firm Artek. [alce::6757588787784107632::p30]

Cited: alce::6757588787784107632::p0, alce::6757588787784107632::p11, alce::6757588787784107632::p14, alce::6757588787784107632::p30

### `2547299592085156135`
> **Electrons are located between the two bonded atoms in a covalent bond**
> Pairs of electrons located between atoms represent covalent bonds. [alce::2547299592085156135::p0]
> In Lewis formulas, electrons are paired between atoms to form covalent bonds, with each single bond made by two valence electrons localized between the two bonded atoms. [alce::2547299592085156135::p15]
> In a polar covalent bond, one or more electrons are unequally shared between two nuclei. [alce::2547299592085156135::p41]

### `941064240300059394`
> **The Byzantine Empire's decline began in the High Middle Ages under pressure from the Seljuk Empire, with the Sack of Constantinople in 1204 marking a significant turning point, though the process of decline is a matter of scholarly debate.**
> The Byzantine Empire following the Sack of Constantinople (1204) slowly collapsed by Ottoman expansion. [alce::941064240300059394::p1]
> In the High Middle Ages, under pressure from the Seljuk Empire, it suffered serious setbacks. [alce::941064240300059394::p1]
> The process by which the empire waned, and from when its decline can be traced, is a matter of scholarly debate. [alce::941064240300059394::p6]
> The collapse of imperial power and authority after 1185 revealed the inadequacy of structural approaches. [alce::941064240300059394::p12]

Cited: alce::941064240300059394::p1, alce::941064240300059394::p12, alce::941064240300059394::p6

### `4653868131958586810`
> **NAT (Network Address Translation) is a method of remapping one IP address space into another by modifying network address information in IP headers**
> Network address translation (NAT) is a method of remapping one IP address space into another by modifying network address information in the IP header of packets while they are in transit across a traffic routing device. [alce::4653868131958586810::p0]
> The technique was originally used as a shortcut to avoid the need to readdress every host when a network was moved, and has become a popular and essential tool in conserving global address space in the face of IPv4 address exhaustion. [alce::4653868131958586810::p0]
> One Internet-routable IP address of a NAT gateway can be used for an entire private network. [alce::4653868131958586810::p0]

Cited: alce::4653868131958586810::p0

### `2900239413928677063`
> **The highest registered air temperature on Earth was recorded in Death Valley, California on June 20, 2013 (and also in Mitribah, Kuwait on July 21, 2016), though the 1913 Death Valley reading of approximately 134°F is historically cited but disputed**
> According to the World Meteorological Organization (WMO), the highest registered air temperature on Earth was in Furnace Creek Ranch, California, located in the Death Valley desert in the United States, on July 10, 1913. [alce::2900239413928677063::p4]
> In January 2012, the WMO decertified the 1922 record, citing persuasive evidence that it was a faulty reading recorded in error by an inexperienced observer. [alce::2900239413928677063::p0]
> If the 1913 record were to be decertified, the highest recorded air temperature on Earth would be recorded both in Death Valley on June 20, 2013, and in Mitribah, Kuwait on July 21, 2016. [alce::2900239413928677063::p7]

Cited: alce::2900239413928677063::p0, alce::2900239413928677063::p4, alce::2900239413928677063::p7

### `-604099956838475859`
> **Benjamin is derived from Hebrew "Binyāmīn", translating as "Son of my right [hand]"**
> Benjamin is a popular given name for males, derived from Hebrew, "Binyāmīn", translating as "Son of my right [hand]". [alce::-604099956838475859::p0]
> According to Genesis, Benjamin was the result of a painful birth in which his mother died, naming him Ben-Oni, "son of my pain," immediately before her death. Instead, Jacob, his father, preferred to call him Benjamin, which can be read in Hebrew as meaning, "son of my right [hand]" (Genesis 35:16-18). [alce::-604099956838475859::p3]
> The name is often shortened to "Ben", and sometimes to "Benny", or "Benji", and like many biblical names, it is popular in the Jewish, Christian and Muslim faiths alike. [alce::-604099956838475859::p0]

Cited: alce::-604099956838475859::p0, alce::-604099956838475859::p3

### `-7338374818994111335`
> **Diffusion occurs in the skin, kidneys, and in amoebas through contractile vacuoles**
> Ammonia is usually expelled directly by diffusion through the skin. [alce::-7338374818994111335::p13]
> Water diffuses out of the cell into the blood stream, from where it is excreted in the form of perspiration, water vapor in breath, or urine from the kidneys. [alce::-7338374818994111335::p45]
> Amoeba makes use of contractile vacuoles to collect excretory wastes, such as ammonia, from the intracellular fluid by diffusion and active transport. [alce::-7338374818994111335::p9]

Cited: alce::-7338374818994111335::p13, alce::-7338374818994111335::p45, alce::-7338374818994111335::p9

### `-285728796551513770`
> **The split was caused by political events and disputes over papal authority, with theological differences playing a secondary role**
> The East–West Schism resulted in the Eastern Orthodox Church and the Catholic Church splitting definitively in 1054, with this fracture caused more by political events than by slight divergences of creed. [alce::-285728796551513770::p9]
> Political causes included popes siding with the king of the Franks, crowning a rival Roman emperor, appropriating the Exarchate of Ravenna, and driving into Greek Italy. [alce::-285728796551513770::p9]
> The schism was sparked in 1054 by Pope Leo IX asserting authority over three of the seats in the Pentarchy in Antioch, Jerusalem, and Alexandria, and by defending the filioque clause in the Nicene Creed. [alce::-285728796551513770::p31]
> The split reflected a larger cultural and political division which had developed in Europe and southwest Asia during the Middle Ages. [alce::-285728796551513770::p33]

Cited: alce::-285728796551513770::p31, alce::-285728796551513770::p33, alce::-285728796551513770::p9

### `-3529909367855134284`
> **Continental crust and oceanic crust**
> Earth has two distinct types of crust: continental crust and oceanic crust. [alce::-3529909367855134284::p2]
> The oceanic crust underlies the ocean basins and is composed of dense (mafic) iron magnesium silicate igneous rocks, like basalt. [alce::-3529909367855134284::p1]
> The continental crust is less dense and composed of (felsic) sodium potassium aluminium silicate rocks, like granite. [alce::-3529909367855134284::p1]

Cited: alce::-3529909367855134284::p1, alce::-3529909367855134284::p2

### `-2578916277348572724`
> **Oral mucosa (mucous membrane)**
> The lining of the mouth is called oral mucosa, which is the mucous membrane lining the inside of the mouth. [alce::-2578916277348572724::p0]
> The oral mucosa consists of stratified squamous epithelium termed oral epithelium and an underlying connective tissue termed lamina propria. [alce::-2578916277348572724::p0]
> The protective tissues of the oral cavity are continuous with the digestive tract and are called mucosa or mucous membranes. [alce::-2578916277348572724::p3]

### `2941611080095900320`
> **Four stars**
> The flag of Chicago consists of two blue horizontal stripes or bars on a field of white, each stripe one-sixth the height of the full flag, and placed slightly less than one-sixth of the way from the top and bottom. Between the two blue stripes are four red, six-pointed stars arranged in a horizontal row. [alce::2941611080095900320::p1]
> Four historical events are commemorated by the four red stars on Chicago's flag: The United States' Fort Dearborn, established at the mouth of the Chicago River in 1803; the Great Chicago Fire of 1871, which destroyed much of the city; the World Columbian Exposition of 1893, by which Chicago celebrated its recovery from the fire; and the Century of Progress World's Fair of 1933–1934, which celebrated the city's centennial. [alce::2941611080095900320::p7]

Cited: alce::2941611080095900320::p1, alce::2941611080095900320::p7

### `-7140472960783987194`
> **The Moon is positioned between the Earth and the Sun during a solar eclipse**
> During a solar eclipse, the Moon is positioned between the Earth and the Sun, blocking the Sun's light from reaching Earth. [alce::-7140472960783987194::p5]
> A solar eclipse is possible only when the Moon is at or near either of the two points where its orbit crosses the ecliptic plane (its orbital nodes). [alce::-7140472960783987194::p20]
> During a solar eclipse, the Moon can sometimes perfectly cover the Sun because its apparent size is nearly the same as the Sun's when viewed from the Earth. [alce::-7140472960783987194::p11]

Cited: alce::-7140472960783987194::p11, alce::-7140472960783987194::p20, alce::-7140472960783987194::p5

### `-8300695731604896849`
> **Multiple contributors developed the first alternating current electric system, with key figures including Hippolyte Pixii (first alternator in 1832), Guillaume Duchenne (1850s), the Hungarian Ganz Works (1870s), and Sebastian Ziani de Ferranti (first practical AC power system in 1886)**
> The first alternator to produce alternating current was a dynamo electric generator based on Michael Faraday's principles constructed by the French instrument maker Hippolyte Pixii in 1832. [alce::-8300695731604896849::p1]
> Alternating current technology had first developed in Europe due to the work of Guillaume Duchenne (1850s), the Hungarian Ganz Works company (1870s), and in the 1880s: Sebastian Ziani de Ferranti, Lucien Gaulard, and Galileo Ferraris. [alce::-8300695731604896849::p0]
> Sebastian Ziani de Ferranti built the world's first practical transformer based alternating current power system at Great Barrington, Massachusetts in 1886. [alce::-8300695731604896849::p6]

Cited: alce::-8300695731604896849::p0, alce::-8300695731604896849::p1, alce::-8300695731604896849::p6

### `-7301225247880986147`
> **The Great Wall of China started building as early as the 7th century BC, with the earliest known construction being the Great Wall of Qi which began in 685 BC during the Spring and Autumn period. Various states built fortifications during the Spring and Autumn (771–476) and Warring States periods (475–221), which were later connected by Qin Shi Huang during the Qin dynasty (221–206 BC).**
> Several walls were being built as early as the 7th century BC; these, later joined together and made bigger and stronger, are collectively referred to as the Great Wall. [alce::-7301225247880986147::p12]
> Construction of the wall started in 685 BC (during the Spring and Autumn period) by the state of Qi, to defend itself against an invasion by the Kingdom of Chu. [alce::-7301225247880986147::p16]
> The history of the Great Wall of China began when fortifications built by various states during the Spring and Autumn (771–476) and Warring States periods (475–221) were connected by the first emperor of China, Qin Shi Huang, to protect his newly founded Qin dynasty (221–206). [alce::-7301225247880986147::p2]

Cited: alce::-7301225247880986147::p12, alce::-7301225247880986147::p16, alce::-7301225247880986147::p2

### `305726888559328341`
> **Metabolic activity occurs in multiple cellular compartments, primarily in the cytosol and mitochondria**
> Most cellular activities take place within the cytoplasm, such as many metabolic pathways including glycolysis, and processes such as cell division. [alce::305726888559328341::p0]
> Major metabolic pathways that occur in the cytosol in animals are protein biosynthesis, the pentose phosphate pathway, glycolysis and gluconeogenesis. [alce::305726888559328341::p1]
> The citric acid cycle, electron transport chain, and oxidative phosphorylation all take place in the mitochondrial membrane, while glycolysis, pentose phosphate pathway, and fatty acid biosynthesis all occur in the cytosol of a cell. [alce::305726888559328341::p9]
> The endoplasm is the site of most cellular processes as it houses the organelles that make up the endomembrane system, and is necessary for most metabolic activities, including cell division. [alce::305726888559328341::p7]

Cited: alce::305726888559328341::p0, alce::305726888559328341::p1, alce::305726888559328341::p7, alce::305726888559328341::p9

### `7803488668042298223`
> **Cupboard love originates from a psychological learning theory of the 1950s-1960s based on psychoanalytic research, describing how infants develop attachment through the mother satisfying their physiological needs, particularly food**
> Cupboard love is a popular learning theory of the 1950s and 1960s based on the research of Sigmund Freud, Anna Freud, Melanie Klein and Mary Ainsworth. [alce::7803488668042298223::p0, alce::7803488668042298223::p2]
> Rooted in psychoanalysis, the theory speculates that attachment develops in the early stages of infancy through the mother satisfying her infant's instinctual needs, exclusively. [alce::7803488668042298223::p2]
> Cupboard love theorists conclude that during infancy, our primary drive is food which leads to a secondary drive for attachment. [alce::7803488668042298223::p0, alce::7803488668042298223::p2]
> The term "cupboard love" is a British idiom, used most commonly in the United Kingdom and Australia, describing the selfish, greedy or insincere affection displayed towards another person in order to get what they want. [alce::7803488668042298223::p1]

Cited: alce::7803488668042298223::p0, alce::7803488668042298223::p1, alce::7803488668042298223::p2

### `5621251439590172784`
> **British PM: Winston Churchill; Viceroy: Lord Wavell**
> In 1942, Gandhi called for the Quit India Movement against the United Kingdom. On the other hand, the Muslim League advised Prime Minister Winston Churchill that Great Britain should 'divide and then Quit'. [alce::5621251439590172784::p18]
> When the Congress Party launched a Quit India Movement of non-violent civil disobedience in 1942, the Raj police immediately arrested tens of thousands of activists (including Gandhi). [alce::5621251439590172784::p70]
> Before assuming the viceroyalty, Lord Wavell had been head of the Indian army and thus had an understanding of the Indian situation. On becoming Viceroy, Wavell's most important task was to present a formula for the future government of India which would be acceptable to both Congress and the Muslim League. [alce::5621251439590172784::p47]
> The viceroy cabled to Winston Churchill that the Quit India movement was 'by far the most serious rebellion since that of 1857'. [alce::5621251439590172784::p3]

### `-8118284230798145822`
> **The Ottoman Empire took over Greece gradually from the mid-15th century, with the process culminating in the Fall of Constantinople in 1453, the conquest of Athens in 1458, and the Despotate of the Morea in 1460. By 1500, most of the plains and islands of Greece were in Ottoman hands.**
> Most of Greece gradually became part of the Ottoman Empire in the late 14th and early 15th century, culminating in the Fall of Constantinople in 1453, the conquest of the Duchy of Athens in 1458, and of the Despotate of the Morea in 1460. [alce::-8118284230798145822::p5]
> By 1500 most of the plains and islands of Greece were in Ottoman hands. [alce::-8118284230798145822::p13]
> This period of Ottoman rule in Greece lasted from the mid-15th century until the successful Greek War of Independence that broke out in 1821. [alce::-8118284230798145822::p19]

### `1261841418358932814`
> **Norway and Denmark**
> Norwegian king Harald Hårfagre annexed the Northern Isles in 875 and Rognvald Eysteinsson received Orkney and Shetland from Harald as an earldom. [alce::1261841418358932814::p3]
> The Kingdom of Norway maintains some claim to territories lost at the dissolution of the Denmark–Norway union, which included the Northern Isles (today part of Scotland). [alce::1261841418358932814::p43]
> On 20 February 1472, the Northern Isles were directly annexed to the Crown of Scotland, and James and his successors fended off all attempts by the Danes to redeem them. [alce::1261841418358932814::p85]

### `-8377155967940203923`
> **Congress determines the size of the Supreme Court**
> Because the Constitution does not specify the Court's size, it was left to Congress to determine the matter through law, and a small number of statutes have legally changed the size of the Court throughout its history. [alce::-8377155967940203923::p3]
> Congress, through the Judiciary Act of 1789, specified the Court's original and appellate jurisdiction, created 13 judicial districts, and fixed the initial size of the Supreme Court at six members. [alce::-8377155967940203923::p2]
> Since the U.S. Constitution does not define the size of the Supreme Court, Roosevelt pointed out that it was within the power of the Congress. [alce::-8377155967940203923::p73]

Cited: alce::-8377155967940203923::p2, alce::-8377155967940203923::p3, alce::-8377155967940203923::p73

### `3055530995120092075`
> **The Navigation Acts and Acts of Trade and Navigation**
> The Navigation Acts, or more broadly The Acts of Trade and Navigation were a long series of English laws that developed, promoted, and regulated English ships, shipping, trade, and commerce between other countries and with its own colonies. [alce::3055530995120092075::p43]
> The English Parliament passed the first of its own mercantilist Navigation Acts (1651) and the Staple Act (1663) that required that English colonial goods be carried only in English ships and legislated limits on trade between the English colonies and foreigners. [alce::3055530995120092075::p1]
> Acts, which were designed to direct most American trade to England (from 1707, Great Britain), and to encourage the manufacture of goods for export to the colonies in Britain. [alce::3055530995120092075::p90]

Cited: alce::3055530995120092075::p1, alce::3055530995120092075::p43, alce::3055530995120092075::p90

> **Christopher G. Memminger (primary author) and a committee of seven members**
> The ordinance was brief and legalistic in nature, containing no explanation of the reasoning behind the delegates' decision: The convention had previously agreed to draft a separate statement that would summarize their justification and gave that task to a committee of seven members comprising Christopher G. Memminger (considered the primary author), F. H. Wardlaw, R. W. Barnwell, J. P. Richardson, B. H. Rutledge, J. E. Jenkins, and P. E. Duncan. [alce::-1016371113458139968::p6]
> When South Carolina seceded from the United States in 1860, Memminger was asked to write the 'Declaration of the Immediate Causes Which Induce and Justify the Secession of South Carolina from the Federal Union' which outlined the reasons for secession. [alce::-1016371113458139968::p0]

### `-3995380877486160814`
> **$25,000 monetary jurisdiction limit**
> The Civil Court of the City of New York is a civil court of the New York State Unified Court System in New York City that decides lawsuits involving claims for damages up to $25,000 and includes a small claims part (small claims court) for cases involving amounts up to $5,000 as well as a housing part (housing court) for landlord-tenant matters. [alce::-3995380877486160814::p3]
> The Civil Court has monetary jurisdiction up to $25,000, including replevin when the value of the chattel does not exceed that amount, real property actions such as partitions, and foreclosures within the monetary limit, and also has equity jurisdiction limited to real property actions, ejectment actions, and actions to rescind or reform a contract not involving more than the $25,000 jurisdictional limit. [alce::-3995380877486160814::p0, alce::-3995380877486160814::p4]

Cited: alce::-3995380877486160814::p0, alce::-3995380877486160814::p3, alce::-3995380877486160814::p4

### `-5980018027276110696`
> **Luke (traditionally believed to be Luke the companion of Paul, though the earliest manuscripts are anonymous)**
> Both the books of Luke and Acts are anonymous narratives written to a man named Theophilus, and almost all scholars believe that they were written by the same person. [alce::-5980018027276110696::p8]
> Traditionally, the text is believed to have been written by Luke the companion of Paul (named in Colossians), however, the earliest manuscripts are anonymous, and the traditional view has been challenged by many modern scholars. [alce::-5980018027276110696::p0]
> The view that Luke-Acts was written by the physician Luke was nearly unanimous in the early Christian church, and nearly all ancient sources shared this theory of authorship—Irenaeus, Tertullian, Clement of Alexandria. [alce::-5980018027276110696::p6]

Cited: alce::-5980018027276110696::p0, alce::-5980018027276110696::p6, alce::-5980018027276110696::p8

### `743600309736467122`
> **1543**

### `8664313149431483137`
> **The musculoskeletal system is an organ system that provides structure, support, stability, and movement to the body through bones, muscles, cartilage, tendons, ligaments, and connective tissue.**
> The human musculoskeletal system is an organ system that gives humans the ability to move using their muscular and skeletal systems. [alce::8664313149431483137::p0]
> The musculoskeletal system provides form, support, stability, and movement to the body. [alce::8664313149431483137::p0]
> It is made up of the bones of the skeleton, muscles, cartilage, tendons, ligaments, joints, and other connective tissue that supports and binds tissues and organs together. [alce::8664313149431483137::p0]
> The musculoskeletal system's primary functions include supporting the body, allowing motion, and protecting vital organs. [alce::8664313149431483137::p0]

> **Paul Revere and William Dawes (and other riders)**

### `5398357621914791303`
> **Baptism originated from water purification rituals found in many cultures, with the Christian practice tracing back to Jesus's baptism by John the Baptist, who adopted baptism as a central sacrament in his messianic movement.**
> The practice of purification via immersion exists in many cultures. [alce::5398357621914791303::p4]
> The Christian ritual of baptism traces back to the baptism of Jesus by John the Baptist, who the Bible says baptized Jesus in the Jordan River. [alce::5398357621914791303::p4]
> John the Baptist adopted baptism as the central sacrament in his messianic movement, seen as a forerunner of Christianity. [alce::5398357621914791303::p1]
> Baptism has been part of Christianity from the start, as shown by the many mentions in the Acts of the Apostles and the Pauline epistles. [alce::5398357621914791303::p1]

Cited: alce::5398357621914791303::p1, alce::5398357621914791303::p4
